title: საქართველოს სისხლის სამართლის კოდექსი — კიბერდანაშაულის დებულებები (მუხლები 284–286)
title_en: Criminal Code of Georgia — Cybercrime Provisions (Articles 284–286)
description: Articles 284 to 286 of the Criminal Code of Georgia establish criminal offenses relating to unauthorized access to computer information, modification of computer data, and interference with computer systems. These provisions implement Georgia's obligations under the Council of Europe Convention on Cybercrime (Budapest Convention), to which Georgia acceded in 2012. The provisions have been interpreted and applied in the context of Georgia's growing digital economy and the increasing threat of cybercrime.

provisions.title: Article 284. Unauthorized Access to Computer Information
provisions.content: Unauthorized access to computer information protected by law, that is, access to information in a computer, computer system, or computer network that is protected by security measures, committed without the authorization of the person entitled to permit such access, shall be punishable by a fine or by imprisonment for a term of up to two years. The same act committed: (a) by an organized group; (b) causing substantial damage; (c) by a person using his/her official status or who has access to a computer, computer system or computer network; (d) targeting critical information infrastructure; shall be punishable by imprisonment for a term of three to five years. The offense of unauthorized access is complete upon the unauthorized entry into the computer system or network, regardless of whether the offender subsequently accesses, copies, or damages any data. Unauthorized access committed from outside the territory of Georgia shall also be within the jurisdiction of Georgian courts where the targeted computer system is located or maintained in Georgia.

provisions.title: Article 285. Modification of Computer Information
provisions.content: Modification, deletion, blocking, or falsification of computer information, that is, the unlawful modification of information stored in electronic form on a computer, in a computer network, or on other media for the storage of electronic information, or the making of false information with the intention that such false information be used as if it were authentic, shall be punishable by a fine or by imprisonment for a term of up to three years. The same act causing substantial damage or committed: (a) against information systems of state agencies; (b) against systems forming part of critical infrastructure; (c) by a person using his/her official status; (d) by an organized group; (e) with the aim of extortion; shall be punishable by imprisonment for a term of four to seven years. The provision applies to any unauthorized alteration of data, including through the use of malware such as ransomware, that prevents the legitimate owner from accessing or using the data in its original state.

provisions.title: Article 286. Creating, Distributing, or Using Harmful Computer Programs
provisions.content: Creating, distributing, selling, or using computer programs or other computer tools designed for the purpose of: (a) gaining unauthorized access to computer information; (b) the unauthorized modification, deletion, or blocking of computer information; (c) rendering ineffective the protective software of a computer system; or (d) intercepting computer communications without authorization; shall be punishable by a fine or by imprisonment for a term of up to three years. The same acts committed: (a) by an organized group; (b) causing substantial damage; (c) resulting in the disruption of critical infrastructure; shall be punishable by imprisonment for a term of four to eight years. This article applies regardless of whether the harmful program is actually used, provided the program is designed primarily for malicious purposes. Possession of harmful programs with intent to distribute is also an offense under this article. A defense is available where the program is created or used for legitimate security research purposes with appropriate authorization.

provisions.title: Article 286-a. Computer Fraud
provisions.content: The obtaining of property or property rights for oneself or for another by way of modifying computer data or interfering with the processing of data or by creating false data with intent to cause loss to another person shall be punishable by a fine or by imprisonment for a term of up to four years. The same act committed: (a) repeatedly; (b) by a group; (c) by a person using his/her official status; (d) on a large scale; (e) targeting banking or payment systems; shall be punishable by imprisonment for a term of five to eight years. Computer fraud includes phishing, unauthorized use of payment credentials, and any other means by which the perpetrator manipulates electronic systems to transfer assets without authorization. This article implements Article 8 of the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime.

provisions.title: Article 286-b. Interference with Computer Systems
provisions.content: The serious hindering, without authorization, of the functioning of a computer system by inputting, transmitting, damaging, deleting, deteriorating, altering or suppressing computer data, including denial of service attacks or other means of making computer systems unavailable to authorized users, shall be punishable by a fine or by imprisonment for a term of up to three years. The same act committed against: (a) critical information infrastructure; (b) state information systems; (c) systems essential for the provision of emergency services; (d) financial infrastructure; shall be punishable by imprisonment for a term of five to ten years. Where the interference results in loss of life or serious physical harm to persons as a result of the unavailability of emergency or medical services, the penalty shall be imprisonment for a term of eight to fifteen years. This article implements Article 5 of the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime.

provisions.title: Article 286-c. Illegal Interception of Computer Communications
provisions.content: The intentional interception, by technical means, of non-public transmissions of computer data to, from, or within a computer system, including electromagnetic emissions from a computer system carrying such computer data, without authorization, shall be punishable by a fine or by imprisonment for a term of up to two years. The same act committed: (a) by a person using his/her official status; (b) for commercial purposes; (c) for political or electoral purposes; (d) against senior officials or members of the armed forces; shall be punishable by imprisonment for a term of three to six years. Authorization may be granted only by a court order issued pursuant to the Law of Georgia on Operative-Investigative Activity or the Criminal Procedure Code of Georgia. The use of unlawfully intercepted computer communications as evidence in civil or criminal proceedings is prohibited.

provisions.title: Article 286-d. Aggravated Cybercrime Offenses
provisions.content: Where any offense under Articles 284 to 286-c is committed: (a) against a foreign state or an international organization or its official; (b) as part of a coordinated attack against multiple targets in Georgia or abroad; (c) by or on behalf of a foreign state or a foreign intelligence service; (d) for the purpose of disrupting elections, referenda, or other democratic processes; (e) against the constitutional order of Georgia; the offense shall be subject to enhanced penalties as provided in this article, including imprisonment for a term not less than five years and up to fifteen years, or in the most serious cases, life imprisonment. Attacks against Georgia's digital infrastructure constituting acts of aggression by a foreign state shall be treated as acts of war and subject to the applicable provisions of international law and the Law on State Security.

provisions.title: Article 286-e. Corporate Criminal Liability
provisions.content: A legal entity shall be held criminally liable for offenses under Articles 284 to 286-d where the offense has been committed on behalf of or in the interest of the legal entity by a natural person who had authority to act on behalf of the legal entity, or by a natural person within the legal entity who was able to commit the offense due to lack of supervision or control by the responsible persons. A legal entity found criminally liable may be subject to: (a) a monetary penalty; (b) forfeiture of instruments and proceeds of the offense; (c) prohibition from engaging in specific commercial activities; (d) judicial supervision; (e) judicial winding-up order. The criminal liability of the legal entity shall not exclude the criminal liability of natural persons who committed the offense, were an accessory thereto, or instigated its commission.

provisions.title: Article 286-f. International Cooperation in Cybercrime Cases
provisions.content: The Prosecutor General's Office of Georgia shall cooperate with foreign law enforcement and judicial authorities in the investigation and prosecution of cybercrime offenses, in accordance with the Budapest Convention on Cybercrime and bilateral mutual legal assistance treaties to which Georgia is party. Such cooperation may include: (a) exchange of information on cybercrime threats and offenders; (b) preservation of electronic evidence pending mutual legal assistance requests; (c) joint investigations with foreign law enforcement; and (d) extradition of suspected offenders. Georgia may exercise jurisdiction over cybercrime offenses where: (a) the offense is committed on Georgian territory; (b) the offense is committed against a Georgian information system; (c) the offender is a Georgian national; or (d) the offense produces effects in Georgia. The 24/7 network contact point for cybercrime established under the Budapest Convention shall be maintained by the Cybercrime Unit of the Prosecutor General's Office.

definitions.definition: Information represented in electronic form, including programs, data, databases, and any other information stored in digital form or processed by a computer system.
Access to a computer system or computer information without the authorization of the person entitled to grant such access, or in excess of any authorization granted.

A computer program or other computer tool designed for the purpose of gaining unauthorized access to computer information, modifying or deleting data without authorization, or intercepting computer communications.
